era: 1981-1989. Post-Punk. E=7 -- art-damaged, angular, blues-inflected post-punk bridging hardcore and alternative rock.

theory_nodes.concept: The Bridge: 1981-1989 span covers exactly the period from hardcore's emergence to grunge's commercial debut. Formed same year as Solger; dissolved same year as Nirvana's Bleach. No other engine band spans this transition so precisely.
theory_nodes.description: Eight years of continuity. The hardware before the software.

theory_nodes.concept: Tom Price cross-membership: Gas Huffer (degree 262) and The U-Men (degree 272). Ten-degree gap. Same guitarist, post-punk to garage rock, Capitol Hill to Capitol Hill across the decade.
theory_nodes.description: Engine's most explicit guitarist continuity across the hardcore-to-alternative arc.

theory_nodes.concept: Art-damage factor: no-wave dissonance, blues intensity (John Bigley), angular riffs (Tom Price). Post-punk as the genre where punk energy meets avant-garde ambition. Stranger and more interesting than conventional rock.
theory_nodes.description: E=7 because more musical information per energy unit than pure hardcore.

cross_references.relationship: TOM PRICE. Degrees 262 and 272: same guitarist, ten degrees apart. U-Men (post-punk, 1981-1989) → Gas Huffer (garage rock, 1990s). Capitol Hill to Capitol Hill.
E=8 to E=7 modulation. The arc shifts from pure hardcore to post-punk. Same Capitol Hill community. Formed the same year (1981 vs 1980).
Same community, same moment. Fartz had Alternative Tentacles and Duff McKagan; U-Men had Black Label and longer career. Both Capitol Hill; different trajectories.

sps_pair.pairing_note: Tactical silence then burst. The U-Men's post-punk uses silence and space deliberately -- the pause before the angular riff arrives. The transient orca uses tactical silence before the burst of speed. Both use strategic silence as a primary tool.
